moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Polish Opening, an unconventional but playable first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside. By advancing the b-pawn, we control c5 and a5, and we're preparing to develop our bishop to b2 where it will aim powerfully at g7 along the long diagonal. This move surprises many opponents unfamiliar with it, and it allows us to establish an unusual but coherent setup that doesn't follow mainstream opening theory.

Black responds solidly by controlling the center from d5 with the e-pawn and preparing to develop pieces harmoniously. This classical French-like setup aims for flexibility and piece mobility; Black isn't committing to a central pawn on d4 immediately, leaving options open for how to structure the position.

We complete a key idea of the Polish Opening by placing our bishop on the long diagonal (a1-h8), where it will pressure Black's center and kingside, particularly eyeing g7. This is the hallmark of the Schiffler-Sokolsky concept: rapid piece coordination before we've even moved our central pawns. The bishop on b2 is perfectly placed to support our queenside expansion.

Black develops a knight to its most natural square, controlling e4 and d5 while preparing kingside castling. This move maintains flexibility and doesn't commit Black to any specific pawn structure yet, which is exactly the balanced approach Black needs against our unconventional setup.

We continue our aggressive queenside plan, advancing the b-pawn to gain space and restrict Black's pieces—particularly targeting the c6 square where Black might want to maneuver a knight or bishop. This aggressive but thematic continuation is characteristic of the Sokolsky variation, pushing the advantage on the wing where we've established early control.

Black strikes at the center, claiming space on d5 and preventing us from easily establishing a pawn there. This is a natural and principled response, taking space in the middle of the board to counterbalance our queenside ambitions and preparing to develop the light-squared bishop with active possibilities.

We prepare to support our center and establish a solid pawn foundation for the middlegame, keeping d4 available for later advancement if needed. Playing e3 also opens the possibility of developing our kingside bishop to d3 or e2, and it keeps our position flexible while maintaining the pressure we've built on the long diagonal with Bb2. We've established a coherent setup: control of c5 and the long diagonal, with space on the queenside and a solid center ready to support our piece play.
